We define as , i.e. 3 rotations (including identity) and 3 reflections. Figure this shit out:

A subgroup H describes symmetries that preserve one chosen object; the cosets of H in G describe the different places that object can be moved by the full symmetry group. Example: a cube symmetry is a square symmetry applied to a face

[TODO] faithful Conjugacy classes denote the same geometric actions, like 120° rotations, etc. For example, see ps1 problem 8.

3.1. Semidirect product

The Euclidean group describes all rigid motions of -dimensional space: combinations of rotations, reflections, and translations. Rotations and translations do not commute.

In 18.701, we proved that an element of is a transformation of the form where and . That is, every Euclidean motion can be decomposed into an orthogonal transformation and a translation. However, we can’t model as the direct product between and , since the rotations and translations don’t commute. In particular, the multiplication structure looks like

(11)

Definition 1 (Internal Semidirect Product). when

  1. is a normal subgroup of and is a subgroup of .

  2. Every can be uniquely written as . Equivalently, the following two conditions hold:

  • This is nice because then we can say:

    (12)

    so multiplcation is defined via conjugation of on .

  • This reduces to a direct product if for all , i.e. and commute. But the converse might not hold? I.e. there could be some different decomposition of those two groups that is a direct product. To prove that is not isomorphic to , we must instead find some group invariant that is violated.

Definition 2 (External Semidirect Product). The external semidirect product of groups and with respect to a homomorphism

(13)

is the group whose underlying set is , with multiplcation

(14)

Both are equivalent up to isomorphism.

This implies every automorphism can be realized as conjugation by some element in a larger group (i.e. just take ).

3.1.1. In rep theory

[TODO] For a direct product, if is an irrep of and is an irrep of , then is an irrep of .

Theorem 2. Every irrep of arises for some irreps of , of . In fact,

the map is a bijection

The representation theory of semi-direct products is more involved than that of direct products — the irreps of the product do not simply factor into irreps of each subgroup.

Remark.
